content:
First, one shows easily by a 2×2‐block‐counting argument that no more than one domino can live in each 2×2 square of the board (because two dominoes in the same 2×2 would necessarily touch side‐to‐side).  Since the 1000×1000 board splits up into 500·500=250 000 disjoint 2×2 blocks, this gives the upper bound  
\[
\#\{\text{dominoes}\}\le250\,000.
\]

Next, one exhibits a periodic “one‐domino‐per‐block’’ placement that actually attains 250 000.  For example, partition the board into 2×2 blocks indexed by (r,c) with 1≤r,c≤500, and in block (r,c) place exactly one horizontal domino:

– If r+c is even, put a domino covering the top two squares of that block.  
– If r+c is odd, put a domino covering the bottom two squares of that block.  

A check shows no two of these dominoes ever share an edge, so this packing is legal and uses exactly one domino in each of the 250 000 blocks.

Hence the maximum possible number of dominoes is

\[
\boxed{250000}.
\]

First, one shows easily by a 2×2‐block‐counting argument that no more than one domino can live in each 2×2 square of the board (because two dominoes in the same 2×2 would necessarily touch side‐to‐side).  Since the 1000×1000 board splits up into 500·500=250 000 disjoint 2×2 blocks, this gives the upper bound  
\[
\#\{\text{dominoes}\}\le250\,000.
\]

Next, one exhibits a periodic “one‐domino‐per‐block’’ placement that actually attains 250 000.  For example, partition the board into 2×2 blocks indexed by (r,c) with 1≤r,c≤500, and in block (r,c) place exactly one horizontal domino:

– If r+c is even, put a domino covering the top two squares of that block.  
– If r+c is odd, put a domino covering the bottom two squares of that block.  

A check shows no two of these dominoes ever share an edge, so this packing is legal and uses exactly one domino in each of the 250 000 blocks.

Hence the maximum possible number of dominoes is

\[
\boxed{250000}.
\]
